words: HATTIE WILKEN JOHNSON:  Camerson area.
SAM SCHRAGER: Is that where you were born?
JOHNSON:  I was born there. Let's see, there was Frank and Mary and Herman, he
was born before I was. They lived down near the Pine Creek and
that was the ranch thai father §ot the homestead. When they went around
and surveyed- those surveyors, they filed on some of and then he
bought father and mother came from Minnesota.
They came on a train they aame on the Great Northern.
And the Northern Pacific, they came from Spokane and then they went
down toward Genesee here,
Eerry they came up on a boat to Lewis ton and
from there up- they brought 'em up here on the hill.
They got to Juliaetta and then came back up here on the hill.

speaker: SS
words: Did most of the boys that went overseas see active duty, fighting in

speaker: SS
words: the war?

speaker: SS
words: JOHNSON:  All of them did. My brother went over there and then later on he

speaker: SS
words: went under Hoover, passing out food, you know. He worked him for

speaker: SS
words: a year before he came back. He didn't come back until 1919, in the

speaker: SS
words: fall. I guess it was September when he came home. And then of course

speaker: SS
words: he had the post office up at Cameron after that. That's when I went

speaker: SS
words: down to Portland to work, 1920, after Christmas. I stayed home for

speaker: SS
words: Christmas then I went down there. Oh, I quite a bit, you know,

speaker: SS
words: I liked to go work someplace where I could earn more money than I

speaker: SS
words: could, you know, close around here. And then the times that I

speaker: SS

speaker: SS
words: thing and she had an operation the last time, and of course, I took

speaker: SS

speaker: SS
words: around; it took til spring before she was able to get up and walk,

speaker: SS
words: not be up, but be in a chair. And then we papered the house and done

speaker: SS

speaker: SS

speaker: SS
words: later on. After I started going to dances- but I went to dances.

speaker: SS

words: And my brother played the accordian and Bill he played the

speaker: SS
words: violin. And, let's see, there was another one, he played the horn.

speaker: SS
words: And it was just, oh, lots of fun, dancing.

words: JOHNSON:  Oh, just all the- they had waltzes and one-stop, two-step and some

speaker: SS
words: times square dances. And then we'd have to try to get the girls to

speaker: SS
words: didn't,

speaker: SS
words: dance, you know. Go look around and those that^try to get the girls

speaker: SS
words: off of their feet.

words: the post office, and they moved the post office a different place and

speaker: SS
words: they called that the hall. And they could dance there, and they kept

speaker: SS

speaker: SS
words: be people come in; these medicine men- have you seen those?

speaker: SS
words: I've heard of 'em.

words: JOHNSON:  They'd come in and peddle their wares and give a show and so on, and

speaker: SS
words: after that was over with they had dances there. Usually kind of to

speaker: SS
words: ward spring, they'd have something like that.

words: How did they keep the floor slick?

speaker: SS
words: JOHNSON:  Oh, they kept it slick- whenever they put that down- I don't know, if

speaker: SS
words: the floor was a little bit rough it didn t make any difference, you

speaker: SS
words: still dance! (Chuckles)

words: Did these guys sell medicine when they put on the show?

speaker: SS
words: JOHNSON:  with the medicine and everything, why they packed up and some

speaker: SS
words: times they'd have someplace to stay.

words: that'd go down, make 'em feel good! (Chuckles) They had several things,

speaker: SS
words: No every time, but sometimes they'd have that outside.

speaker: SS

speaker: SS

speaker: SS

words: went—

words: Yeah, 1917 and then it ended in 1918. But I was thinking; I wonder

speaker: SS
words: because it went on so long.

speaker: SS

speaker: SS
words: of the English- some of the other people you know, that weren't German.

speaker: SS
words: A lot of them they tried to talk down they could take the

speaker: SS
words: land away from the German people here and so on. They were real snob

speaker: SS
words: bish or snotty that way. But it didn't work out that way. They went

speaker: SS
words: right on a working. And of course, they couldn't hear from Germany.

speaker: SS

words: JOHNSON:  Huh?

speaker: SS

speaker: SS

words: you know, they were called on and they had to obey the laws here.
